title: Branching and Loops in ARM Assembly
summary: Conditional and unconditional branches, loops, subroutine calls, and calling conventions in ARM.
content_html:
Branching and Loops in ARM Assembly

Sequential execution is not enough to write useful programs — we need to make decisions and repeat operations. In ARM assembly, branch instructions redirect program execution to different locations based on conditions. Combined with comparison instructions that set the CPSR flags, branches implement all control flow structures: if-statements, while loops, for loops, and subroutine calls.

Unconditional Branch: B

The simplest branch instruction is B (Branch), which jumps unconditionally to a label:

  • B loop_start — jump immediately to the label loop_start
  • The assembler calculates the offset from the current PC to the target label and encodes it in the instruction
  • Range: approximately ±32 MB (24-bit signed offset, scaled by 4)
  • B changes the PC to the target address; execution continues from there

Conditional Branches

Conditional branches check the CPSR flags and jump only if the condition is true. They are always used after a CMP instruction or an arithmetic instruction with the S suffix (ADDS, SUBS, etc.):

  • BEQ label — Branch if Equal (Z=1). Used after CMP to test equality.
  • BNE label — Branch if Not Equal (Z=0)
  • BGT label — Branch if Greater Than (signed, Z=0 and N=V)
  • BLT label — Branch if Less Than (signed, N ≠ V)
  • BGE label — Branch if Greater Than or Equal (signed, N=V)
  • BLE label — Branch if Less Than or Equal (signed, Z=1 or N ≠ V)
  • BHI label — Branch if Higher (unsigned)
  • BLO label — Branch if Lower (unsigned)

Implementing a While Loop

A while loop in ARM assembly follows this pattern: set up the counter, check the condition at the top, branch out when done, execute the body, increment, then branch back to the check.

For example, a loop that sums values 1 to 10:

  1. MOV R0, #0 (accumulator = 0)
  2. MOV R1, #1 (counter i = 1)
  3. loop: CMP R1, #11 (compare i with 11)
  4. BGE done (if i ≥ 11, exit loop)
  5. ADD R0, R0, R1 (sum += i)
  6. ADD R1, R1, #1 (i++)
  7. B loop (go back to check)
  8. done: ... (continue after loop)

Implementing a For Loop

A for loop is similar — the counter initialisation and increment are just structured differently in the source code, but the assembly structure is identical. A count-down loop (common optimisation) compares with zero, which may be slightly faster since SUBS already sets the Z flag.

Subroutine Calls: BL and BX LR

Subroutines (functions) in ARM assembly use two special instructions:

  • BL label (Branch with Link) — calls a subroutine. It does two things: copies the return address (PC+4, the instruction after BL) into the Link Register (LR = R14), then branches to the label.
  • BX LR (Branch and Exchange to LR) — returns from a subroutine by loading LR back into PC. This transfers execution back to the instruction after the BL call.

ARM Calling Conventions (AAPCS)

The AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard) defines conventions that all ARM code should follow so that functions written in different languages can call each other:

  • R0-R3: Argument registers. The first four function arguments go in R0-R3 in order. Return value goes in R0 (64-bit result uses R0-R1).
  • R4-R11: Callee-saved registers. If a function uses these, it must save them on the stack and restore them before returning.
  • R12 (IP): Scratch register, can be modified freely.
  • R13 (SP): Must be 8-byte aligned at all times and must be restored before returning.
  • R14 (LR): Caller saves this if it needs to call another function.

Preserving Registers with PUSH/POP

When a subroutine uses callee-saved registers (R4-R11) or calls other subroutines (overwriting LR), it must save and restore them using the stack:

  • At function entry: PUSH {R4, R5, LR} — saves R4, R5, and LR
  • At function exit: POP {R4, R5, PC} — restores R4, R5, and pops the saved LR directly into PC to return

Nested Subroutine Calls

If function A calls function B, and B calls function C, the LR register is overwritten each time BL is used. The solution is to push LR to the stack before calling a deeper function:

  1. Function A: PUSH {LR} before calling B
  2. BL B — LR is overwritten with A’s return address (but it’s saved on stack)
  3. When A returns: POP {PC} — restores the saved LR into PC

Without saving LR, the return from B would overwrite LR with B’s return address, and A would never know how to get back to its caller.

Practical Example: Factorial

Computing factorial recursively in ARM assembly demonstrates all these concepts: the function checks the base case (CMP + BEQ), calls itself recursively (BL), and preserves registers (PUSH/POP). Each recursive call saves its LR on the stack, building up a call chain that unwinds correctly as each call returns with BX LR or POP PC.

exercises.title: BL Instruction Effect
exercises.prompt: When ARM executes 'BL myFunc', what happens to the Link Register (R14)?
options: It is set to the address of myFunc
It is set to the address of the instruction after BL
It holds the stack pointer value
exercises.answer: It is set to the address of the instruction after BL

exercises.title: Returning from Subroutine
exercises.prompt: Which instruction is the standard way to return from a simple ARM subroutine?

exercises.title: Conditional Branch After CMP
exercises.prompt: After 'CMP R0, #5', which branch instruction will jump only if R0 is less than 5 (signed)?

exercises.title: Nested Call LR Problem
exercises.prompt: Why must a subroutine that calls another subroutine save LR to the stack before the call?
options: BL overwrites LR with the callee's address, losing the caller's return address
LR cannot hold addresses larger than 16 bits
The stack automatically clears LR on each call
BX LR requires LR to be zero before use
exercises.answer: BL overwrites LR with the callee's address, losing the caller's return address
